walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Main Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get seven Wonders Duel set up. First, place the main board between both players. This is the long rectangular board with the military track running across it. The track has a row of spaces with a conflict pawn icon in the center. Make sure both players can comfortably reach the board.

walkthrough.title: Place the Conflict Pawn
walkthrough.text: Now find the shield-shaped conflict pawn. Place it right on the center space of the military track. This pawn will move toward one player or the other as military cards are played throughout the game. If it ever reaches a player's capital at the far end, that player loses immediately.

walkthrough.title: Place Military Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the military tokens. These are the round tokens with coin penalties on them. Place them on the marked threshold spaces on the military track. There are two thresholds on each side of the center. When the conflict pawn crosses a threshold, the defending player loses coins as shown on that token.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Progress Tokens
walkthrough.text: Let's set up the progress tokens next. Find all ten round progress tokens with green symbols on them. Shuffle them face down, then randomly select five and place them face up on the marked spaces of the board. Return the other five to the box without looking at them.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Age Decks
walkthrough.text: Now let's organize the card decks. Separate all the cards into three piles by their card backs. Age one cards have a single dot, Age two cards have two dots, and Age three cards have three dots. The purple Guild cards also have an Age three back but are marked with a purple border. Set the Guild cards aside for now.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Age One Deck
walkthrough.text: Take the Age one deck and shuffle it. Then randomly remove three cards face down and return them to the box without looking. You should have twenty Age one cards remaining. These hidden cards add uncertainty so you never know exactly what is available each game.

walkthrough.title: Build the Age One Card Structure
walkthrough.text: Now arrange the twenty Age one cards in a pyramid structure on the table. Start with a row of six cards at the bottom, then five overlapping cards above that, then four, then three, then two at the top. Cards in odd rows are face up and cards in even rows are face down. A card is only available to take when no other cards are covering it.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Wonder Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the twelve Wonder cards. These are the large cards with famous ancient structures on them. Shuffle all twelve face down. You will use eight of them this game, four per player. The rest go back in the box.

walkthrough.title: Draft Wonders — First Round
walkthrough.text: Let's do the Wonder draft. Choose a starting player randomly. Deal four Wonder cards face up on the table. The first player picks one, then the second player picks two, then the first player takes the last one. Each player now has two Wonders.

walkthrough.title: Draft Wonders — Second Round
walkthrough.text: Deal four more Wonder cards face up. This time the second player picks first. The second player picks one, then the first player picks two, then the second player takes the last one. Each player should now have four Wonders in front of them. This snake draft keeps things fair.

walkthrough.title: Give Starting Coins
walkthrough.text: Each player takes seven coins from the bank. The coin tokens come in denominations of one, three, and six. Keep your coins visible in front of you. Your coin total is always public information.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: The player who was first in the Wonder draft also takes the first turn of the game. That player will choose the first card from the Age one pyramid. Once you have played through all twenty cards in the pyramid, Age one ends and you will set up Age two. Let's learn the rules.

summary.title: Draft Wonders — Second Round

Each player now has 4 Wonders

summary.title: Give Starting Coins
bullets: Each player takes 7 coins from the bank

summary.title: Determine First Player
bullets: First Wonder drafter goes first in Age I
Age ends when all 20 cards are taken from the structure

walkthrough.title: Taking a Card
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how a turn works. On your turn, you must take one available card from the card structure. A card is available when no other card is overlapping it from the row below. When you take a face-down card that was hidden, you immediately flip it face up and it becomes available on a future turn.

walkthrough.title: Three Choices for Your Card
walkthrough.text: Once you take an available card, you have three choices. You can build the card by paying its resource cost and adding it to your city. You can discard the card to gain coins equal to two plus the number of yellow cards in your city. Or you can use the card to build one of your Wonders by tucking it under a Wonder card and paying that Wonder's cost.

walkthrough.title: Paying Resource Costs
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about paying for cards. Each card shows its cost in the upper left corner. Brown and grey resource cards you have already built produce resources for free. If you are missing a resource, you can buy it from the bank for two coins per unit. However, for each matching resource card your opponent has, the price increases by one coin per unit.

walkthrough.title: Chain Building
walkthrough.text: Some cards have a small symbol in the upper left showing a chain link to another card. If you already own the card shown in the chain symbol, you can build the new card for free regardless of its normal cost. Chain building is one of the most efficient moves in the game, so keep an eye out for these connections.

walkthrough.title: Brown Cards — Raw Resources
walkthrough.text: Let's go through the card types. Brown cards produce raw resources like wood, stone, clay, and ore. When you build a brown card, place it in your city. It permanently produces that resource for you, meaning you never have to pay for it again. Some brown cards produce two units of a single resource or one unit of two different resources.

walkthrough.title: Grey Cards — Manufactured Goods
walkthrough.text: Grey cards produce manufactured goods, which are papyrus and glass. These work just like brown cards but tend to be rarer and more expensive to buy from the bank. Many powerful cards in Ages two and three require manufactured goods, so securing these early can save you a lot of coins later.

walkthrough.title: Red Cards — Military
walkthrough.text: Red cards give you military strength shown by shield icons. When you build a red card, immediately move the conflict pawn one space toward your opponent for each shield on the card. If the pawn crosses a threshold on your opponent's side, they must pay the coin penalty shown on that token and the token is removed. If the pawn reaches your opponent's capital space, you win immediately by military supremacy.

walkthrough.title: Green Cards — Science
walkthrough.text: Green cards display scientific symbols. There are seven different symbols in the game. When you collect a matching pair of the same symbol, you immediately choose one of the five available progress tokens from the board. These tokens give powerful ongoing bonuses. If you ever collect six different scientific symbols, you win immediately by scientific supremacy.

walkthrough.title: Blue Cards — Victory Points
walkthrough.text: Blue cards are the simplest type. They are worth victory points shown directly on the card and have no special ability. They do not do anything during the game, but they contribute to your final score at the end. Blue cards often have high point values, making them important for a balanced scoring strategy.

walkthrough.title: Yellow Cards — Commerce
walkthrough.text: Yellow cards give you economic advantages. Some yellow cards reduce the cost of buying resources from the bank. Others give you coins immediately or provide resources that work like brown or grey cards. A few yellow cards are worth victory points at the end of the game based on conditions in your city.

walkthrough.title: Purple Cards — Guilds
walkthrough.text: Purple Guild cards only appear in Age three. Before building the Age three structure, shuffle three random Guild cards into the Age three deck and remove the rest. Guild cards are worth victory points based on what both you and your opponent have built. For example, a Guild might score points for each brown card between both cities.

walkthrough.title: Building Wonders
walkthrough.text: Instead of building or discarding a card, you can tuck it under one of your four Wonder cards to construct that Wonder. You must pay the Wonder's resource cost, not the tucked card's cost. The tucked card is hidden and does not count as part of your city. Each Wonder gives a unique bonus when built. Only seven of the eight total Wonders between both players can be built in a game.

walkthrough.title: End of an Age
walkthrough.text: When all twenty cards in the current structure have been taken, the Age ends. For Age two, shuffle the Age two deck, remove three random cards, and build a new card structure. The same goes for Age three, except you also shuffle in the Guild cards. The player with the conflict pawn on their opponent's side goes first in the next Age.

walkthrough.title: Winning by Supremacy
walkthrough.text: There are two ways to win instantly during the game. Military supremacy happens when the conflict pawn reaches your opponent's capital on the far end of the track. Scientific supremacy happens when you collect six of the seven different science symbols. If either of these happens, the game ends immediately and that player wins.

walkthrough.title: End Game Scoring
walkthrough.text: If nobody wins by supremacy, the game ends after Age three. Add up victory points from all sources: blue cards, green cards, yellow cards, purple Guild cards, Wonders, progress tokens, your position on the military track, and coins. For coins, you earn one victory point for every three coins you have. The player with the most points wins.

walkthrough.title: Military Track Scoring
walkthrough.text: At end game, check where the conflict pawn sits on the military track. The space it occupies shows how many victory points the leading military player earns. The values range from zero to ten depending on how far the pawn pushed into enemy territory. The losing player on the military track earns zero military points.

walkthrough.title: Tiebreaker
walkthrough.text: If both players end with the same total victory points, the tiebreaker goes to the player with the most victory points from blue civilian cards alone. If still tied, the game is a draw. Now let's try a practice round to see everything in action.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Practice Game
walkthrough.text: Let's play through a practice game of seven Wonders Duel together. We will walk through the key moments of a game between two players, Alex and Sam. We will skip ahead to the interesting decisions so you can see how the game flows. Alex will go first this game.

walkthrough.title: Alex's First Turn — Grabbing Resources
walkthrough.text: Let's start Age one. Alex looks at the available cards in the pyramid and sees a Lumber Yard, which is a brown card producing one wood. Alex builds it for free since brown cards in Age one have no cost. Smart move. Resources in Age one are the foundation of everything you build later.

walkthrough.title: Sam's First Turn — A Grey Card
walkthrough.text: Sam spots a Glassworks, a grey card producing one glass. Sam builds it for free as well. Grey cards are even more valuable than brown cards because manufactured goods are harder to come by. Many powerful Age two and Age three cards need papyrus or glass, so grabbing them early is a great investment.

walkthrough.title: A Few Turns In — Building Up
walkthrough.text: After a few more turns, Alex has picked up a Stone Pit and a Clay Pool, giving Alex wood, stone, and clay. Sam has glass plus a Quarry for stone. Both players are building a resource base that will let them afford more expensive cards in later Ages.

walkthrough.title: Sam Spots a Science Card
walkthrough.text: Sam sees a Pharmacist, a green science card with a mortar and pestle symbol. Sam builds it by paying the required resource. This is Sam's first science symbol. Remember, collecting a pair of the same symbol earns a progress token, and six different symbols wins the game outright.

walkthrough.title: Mistake — Alex Ignores the Science Threat
walkthrough.text: Alex sees another green card with the same mortar and pestle symbol become available. Instead of taking it to block Sam, Alex grabs a blue card worth three victory points. Alex thinks the blue points are more valuable right now. But leaving that matching science symbol for Sam could be dangerous.

walkthrough.title: Lesson — Always Watch for Science Pairs
walkthrough.text: Sam immediately takes the second mortar and pestle card and earns a progress token. Sam picks the Law token, which counts as any science symbol. Now Sam effectively has three unique symbols toward the six needed for scientific supremacy. The lesson here is to always block your opponent from completing science pairs, even if it means skipping a card you want.

walkthrough.title: Alex Plays a Military Card
walkthrough.text: Alex decides to push the military track. Alex builds a Guard Tower, a red card with one shield. The conflict pawn moves one space toward Sam. This is a good way to apply pressure. If Sam ignores the military, Alex could push the pawn across a threshold and cost Sam coins.

walkthrough.title: Transitioning to Age Two
walkthrough.text: All twenty cards from the Age one pyramid have been taken. Let's set up Age two. We shuffle the Age two deck, remove three random cards, and arrange a new twenty-card structure. Since Alex has the military advantage, Alex goes first in Age two.

walkthrough.title: Mistake — Alex Overpays for Resources
walkthrough.text: Alex wants to build a Forum, a yellow card. It costs one clay, but Alex already used his Clay Pool. Alex decides to buy clay from the bank. Normally that would cost two coins, but Sam has a Quarry that produces stone, not clay, so Alex thinks it costs two. However, Alex forgot that Sam also has a Clay Reserve from an earlier turn. The real cost is three coins because Sam has one matching resource.

walkthrough.title: Lesson — Track Your Opponent's Resources
walkthrough.text: When buying resources from the bank, always check what your opponent produces. Each matching resource your opponent has adds one coin to your purchase price. Alex could have saved coins by building a different card or by having built more resource cards in Age one. Knowing your opponent's city is just as important as knowing your own.

walkthrough.title: Sam Builds a Wonder
walkthrough.text: Sam has a strong resource base and decides to build a Wonder. Sam takes an available card from the structure and tucks it under The Great Lighthouse. Sam pays the Wonder's cost of one wood and one stone. The Great Lighthouse lets Sam produce one of any raw resource on future turns. Notice that the tucked card is gone. It does not matter what it was.

walkthrough.title: Alex Uses Chain Building
walkthrough.text: Alex spots a Library card in the structure. It normally costs two stone and one glass. But Alex already has an Apothecary from Age one that chains into the Library. Thanks to the chain symbol, Alex builds the Library for free. That is a huge savings. Always look for chain icons on your existing cards when deciding what to build.

walkthrough.title: Mistake — Sam Ignores Military Pressure
walkthrough.text: Alex has been steadily playing red cards and the conflict pawn is getting close to the first threshold on Sam's side. Sam has focused entirely on science and blue cards without playing any military. Alex plays a Barracks card with two shields, pushing the pawn across the first threshold. Sam loses two coins.

walkthrough.title: Lesson — Do Not Neglect Military Entirely
walkthrough.text: Even if military supremacy is not your main strategy, you need to play at least a few red cards to keep the conflict pawn from reaching your side. Losing coins to threshold penalties hurts your ability to build expensive cards. A couple of military cards can keep the pawn near the center and protect your economy.

walkthrough.title: Entering Age Three
walkthrough.text: Age two is complete. We shuffle the Age three deck and add three random Guild cards before removing three random cards and building the structure. The stakes are higher now. Age three cards give the most points, and both supremacy conditions are easier to trigger with more cards in play.

walkthrough.title: Sam Completes a Fourth Wonder
walkthrough.text: Sam builds a second Wonder this Age, bringing Sam's total to three Wonders built. Sam has one more Wonder to go. Remember, only seven total Wonders can be built across both players. Alex has built two Wonders so far, so if Alex does not act quickly, Sam could lock Alex out of building a fourth.

walkthrough.title: Alex Grabs a Guild Card
walkthrough.text: Alex takes a Builders Guild card, which scores two victory points for each Wonder built in the game, across both cities. Since five Wonders have been built between both players, this card is already worth ten points. Guild cards can be massive point swings, so evaluate them carefully in Age three.

walkthrough.title: Final Turns and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The last few cards are taken from the structure. Neither player achieved military or scientific supremacy, so we go to final scoring. Let's add everything up. Blue cards, green card points, yellow card points, Guild card points, Wonder bonuses, progress token points, military track position, and one point for every three coins.

walkthrough.title: The Final Score
walkthrough.text: Alex finishes with fifty-four points, boosted by the Builders Guild and solid military scoring. Sam finishes with fifty-one points, with strong science progress tokens and Wonders but losing ground on military penalties. Alex wins by three points. A close game where small decisions made all the difference.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check General Tips → strategy basics and common mistakes
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for card types and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Grey Cards Early
walkthrough.text: Let's go over some general tips for seven Wonders Duel. Manufactured goods, papyrus and glass, are the most important resources in the game. Many powerful Age two and Age three cards require them, and buying them from the bank gets expensive fast. If you see a grey card available in Age one, strongly consider taking it.

walkthrough.title: Watch Both Supremacy Threats
walkthrough.text: Always keep an eye on both the military track and your opponent's science collection. It is easy to get tunnel vision chasing your own strategy and suddenly lose to a supremacy condition you did not see coming. Every couple of turns, glance at the conflict pawn and count your opponent's green card symbols.

walkthrough.title: Use Chain Building Whenever Possible
walkthrough.text: Chain building is the most efficient way to play expensive cards. A card that normally costs three resources can be built for free if you own the prerequisite. When choosing between two similarly valuable cards, pick the one that chains into something useful in the next Age. The chain icons are shown in the lower left of each card.

walkthrough.title: Discarding Is a Legitimate Strategy
walkthrough.text: Do not overlook discarding a card for coins. Sometimes the best move is to deny your opponent a powerful card while padding your coin supply. The payout is two coins plus the number of yellow commerce cards you have built. With three or four yellow cards, a discard can net you five or six coins.

walkthrough.title: Time Your Wonder Builds
walkthrough.text: Only seven of eight total Wonders can be built in a game. If your opponent has built three Wonders and you have only built one, you need to act quickly before they build their fourth and lock out one of yours. Wonders with extra turn bonuses are especially powerful because they let you take two actions in a row.

walkthrough.title: Pay Attention to Face-Down Cards
walkthrough.text: When you take a card that reveals a face-down card beneath it, you are giving your opponent new options. Think about whether uncovering a hidden card might help your opponent more than the card you are taking helps you. Sometimes it is better to take a card from a different part of the structure.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Scoring
walkthrough.text: The strongest games usually score points from multiple sources. Relying entirely on blue cards or entirely on military leaves you vulnerable. A mix of blue points, military track position, Wonder bonuses, and a few well-timed Guilds gives you the most consistent path to victory.

walkthrough.title: Count the Remaining Cards
walkthrough.text: In the later stages of each Age, you can see most of the remaining cards. Count what is left and plan your last few turns carefully. Think about which cards your opponent needs and whether you can deny them. This is especially important in Age three when every decision could determine the winner.

summary.title: Prioritize Grey Cards Early
bullets: Papyrus and glass are scarce and widely needed in Ages II/III
Buy early to avoid expensive bank purchases later

summary.title: Watch Both Supremacy Threats
bullets: Check military track + opponent's science symbols regularly
Tunnel vision → surprise supremacy loss

summary.title: Use Chain Building Whenever Possible
bullets: Free builds save 2-5 coins worth of resources
Chain icons in lower-left of cards → check for links across Ages

summary.title: Discarding Is a Legitimate Strategy
bullets: Discard = 2 + # yellow cards in coins
Denies opponent a card + funds your economy

summary.title: Time Your Wonder Builds
bullets: Only 7 of 8 Wonders can be built — race matters
Extra-turn Wonders are especially powerful

summary.title: Pay Attention to Face-Down Cards
bullets: Revealing hidden cards creates new options for opponent
Consider which part of the structure to take from

summary.title: Diversify Your Scoring
bullets: Mix blue VP + military + Wonders + Guilds for consistency
Single-source strategies are easier to counter

summary.title: Count the Remaining Cards
bullets: Late in each Age, most cards are visible → plan ahead
Deny opponent key cards in Age III for maximum impact

walkthrough.title: The Science Supremacy Threat
walkthrough.text: Let's dive into advanced strategies. The science supremacy win is a constant pressure tool even if you do not plan to complete it. Collecting three or four different symbols forces your opponent to hate-draft green cards just to block you. This means they are spending turns on cards they may not want, giving you an indirect advantage even if you never reach six symbols.

walkthrough.title: Progress Token Selection
walkthrough.text: When you earn a progress token from a science pair, choose wisely. The Agriculture token gives you six coins and four victory points, making it the safest choice. The Strategy token gives you extra shield strength and is devastating in a military-focused game. The Economy token lets you earn coins when your opponent buys resources you produce, which can generate a fortune over time.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Card Structure
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about the card structure as a puzzle. Taking a card on one side of the pyramid reveals different cards than taking from the other side. Sometimes the best play is not the most valuable available card, but the card that reveals something you want underneath while keeping a powerful card buried for your opponent.

walkthrough.title: The Military Yo-Yo
walkthrough.text: Once the conflict pawn crosses a threshold, that penalty token is removed permanently. This means pushing the pawn back and forth does not re-trigger penalties. Smart players use this by intentionally letting the pawn come back to center after the first threshold, then pushing it forward again to cross the second threshold. This maximizes coin damage to your opponent.

walkthrough.title: Wonder Denial and Timing
walkthrough.text: Since only seven Wonders can be built, building your fourth Wonder before your opponent builds their third is a huge power move. It forces them to choose which three of four Wonders to actually build. Wonders that grant an extra turn are best used when a critical card is about to become available, letting you take two key cards in a row.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponent's Strategy
walkthrough.text: Pay close attention to what your opponent builds in Age one. If they are stockpiling science cards, be ready to block pairs. If they are building red cards, invest in a couple of defensive military plays. If they are hoarding resources without building much, they are likely preparing for expensive Age two and three plays. Adapt your strategy based on what you see.

walkthrough.title: The Discard Economy Engine
walkthrough.text: With three or four yellow commerce cards, discarding becomes extremely profitable. You can discard cards your opponent needs while earning five or six coins per discard. Use that wealth to buy resources from the bank for expensive Age three cards or Wonders. This strategy turns denial into fuel for your own engine.

walkthrough.title: Guild Card Planning
walkthrough.text: In Age three, only three of the possible Guild cards appear. If you have been tracking what both players have built, you can estimate which Guilds will be most valuable. A Traders Guild is amazing if there are lots of yellow cards in play. A Scientists Guild shines when many green cards are on the table. Plan your Age three turns to grab the best-scoring Guild before your opponent does.

summary.title: The Science Supremacy Threat
bullets: 3-4 unique symbols forces opponent to hate-draft green cards
Indirect advantage even without completing supremacy

summary.title: Progress Token Selection
bullets: Agriculture: 6 coins + 4 VP (safest)
Strategy: extra shield power (military combo)
Economy: earn coins when opponent buys your resources

summary.title: Controlling the Card Structure
bullets: Take cards to reveal what you want, bury what opponent wants
Think of the pyramid as a positional puzzle

summary.title: The Military Yo-Yo
bullets: Threshold tokens are removed permanently once crossed
Push → let pawn return → push again to hit 2nd threshold
Maximizes coin damage over time

summary.title: Wonder Denial and Timing
bullets: Build 4th Wonder before opponent builds 3rd → lock them out
Extra-turn Wonders: use when critical card is about to appear

summary.title: Reading Your Opponent's Strategy
bullets: Age I choices reveal opponent's plan → adapt accordingly
Science hoarder → block pairs; Military buildup → invest in defense

summary.title: The Discard Economy Engine
bullets: 3-4 yellow cards → 5-6 coins per discard
Deny opponent's key cards while funding your own builds

summary.title: Guild Card Planning
bullets: Only 3 Guilds appear in Age III — track both cities to predict value
Grab highest-scoring Guild before opponent

entries.definition: Produces raw resources (wood, stone, clay, ore). Free in Age I. Resources are permanent and reduce bank purchase costs.
Produces manufactured goods (papyrus or glass). Rarer than brown cards. Many expensive Age II and III cards require these.
Military card with shield icons. Each shield moves the conflict pawn one space toward your opponent on the military track.
Science card displaying one of seven unique symbols. Pairs earn progress tokens. Six different symbols triggers scientific supremacy.
Civilian card worth flat victory points at end of game. No in-game effect. Used as tiebreaker category.
Commerce card providing economic advantages: reduced bank costs, coin income, resource production, or end-game VP bonuses.
Available only in Age III. Scores VP based on conditions across both players' cities. Three random Guilds are shuffled into each game.
Shield-shaped piece on the military track. Starts at center. Moves toward a player when their opponent plays military cards. Reaching the capital triggers military supremacy.
Board showing spaces from one player's capital to the other. Contains threshold spaces with coin penalty tokens. Position determines military VP at end of game (0-10).
Instant win condition triggered when the conflict pawn reaches the opponent's capital space at the far end of the military track.
Coin penalty placed on the military track. When the conflict pawn crosses it, the defending player loses the indicated coins. Token is then removed permanently.
Instant win condition triggered when a player collects six of the seven different science symbols. Can come from green cards, progress tokens, or Wonders.
Two matching science symbols. Completing a pair lets you immediately choose one of the face-up progress tokens from the board.
Powerful bonus tile earned by completing science pairs. Five are available each game from a pool of ten. Effects include VP, coins, resource discounts, and special abilities.
A progress token that counts as any one science symbol. Effectively gives you a wild symbol toward scientific supremacy.
Large card representing an ancient wonder. Each has a unique cost and powerful bonus. Built by tucking any available age card underneath and paying the Wonder's cost.
Setup phase where 8 Wonders are distributed. Two rounds of snake drafting: 1-2-1, then 2-1-2. Ensures fair distribution of powerful Wonders.
Only seven of the eight total Wonders between both players can be built in a single game. The eighth remains unbuilt. Creates a race to build before being locked out.
Some Wonders grant an extra turn when built, allowing the player to take two consecutive actions. Powerful for grabbing critical cards back-to-back.
Buying a resource you do not produce. Base cost is 2 coins per unit, plus 1 coin for each matching resource your opponent produces. Yellow commerce cards can reduce this cost.
Instead of building, discard a card to earn coins equal to 2 plus the number of yellow cards in your city. Useful for denying opponents and funding future builds.
Building a card for free by owning its prerequisite card. Chain symbols appear in the lower-left of cards. Chains typically connect across Ages (Age I card chains to Age II card).
A progress token that earns you coins whenever your opponent buys a resource you produce from the bank. Can generate significant income over time.
Total VP from: blue cards + green cards + yellow cards + purple Guilds + Wonders + progress tokens + military track + coins (1 VP per 3 coins). Highest total wins.
At end of game, every 3 coins in your supply is worth 1 VP. Remainder is ignored. Coins are always public information.
If VP totals are equal, the player with the most VP from blue civilian cards wins. If still tied, the game is a draw.
A card in the structure that is not partially covered by any card in the row below. Only available cards can be taken on a player's turn.
The pyramid-like arrangement of 20 cards each Age. Cards overlap in alternating face-up and face-down rows. Taking cards reveals hidden ones beneath.
The game is played over three Ages. Each Age uses its own deck with increasingly powerful cards. Age III includes Guild cards. A new 20-card structure is built each Age.
The collection of cards a player has built in front of them. Cards in your city produce resources, provide military strength, display science symbols, and score VP.
A drafting method where pick order reverses each round. In 7 Wonders Duel, Wonders are drafted 1-2-1 then 2-1-2 to balance first-pick advantage.
